title: Step 1: Copy the string listed under the  heading.

sections.heading: HTTP (HyperText Transport Protocol) Methods
sections.body:
All resources support a subset of these common HTTP (HyperText Transport Protocol) Methods:

| Method | Purpose                                                                                                        |
| ------ | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| GET    | Retrieve the JSON (Javascript Object Notation) representation of a resource.                                   |
| POST   | Create a new resource using the provided JSON (Javascript Object Notation) representation.                     |
| PUT    | Replace a resource with the provided JSON (Javascript Object Notation) representation.                         |
| PATCH  | Update the specified fields in a resource using the provided JSON (Javascript Object Notation) representation. |
| DELETE | Remove a resource.                                                                                             |
| HEAD   | Returns the response header without the JSON (Javascript Object Notation) representation of the resource.      |

sections.heading: JSON (Javascript Object Notation)
sections.body:
All entities are represented in JSON (Javascript Object Notation). The following rules and
conventions apply:

- **Content Type Request Header**

  When sending JSON (Javascript Object Notation) to the server via `POST` or `PUT`, make sure
  to specify the correct content type request header: `Content-Type:
  application/json`.

- **Invalid Fields**

  Invalid fields are *rejected* rather than *ignored*. For example,
  if you attempt to create a new entity and misspell one of the fields, or
  attempt to update an existing entity and include a field that
  can't be modified, the server responds with a 400 status code and an
  error message stating which field is invalid.

- **ISO-8601-Formatted Dates**

  All dates are returned as [ISO-8601](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISO8601)-formatted strings designated in
  UTC. When sending dates to the server (i.e., as query parameters or
  fields in `POST` or `PATCH` request entities), use
  ISO-8601-formatted dates. If you do not specify a time zone, UTC is
  assumed. However, it is highly recommended that you include a time
  zone designator to avoid any ambiguity.

- **BSON Timestamps**

  In some cases, a timestamp is returned as a [BSON timestamp](manual/reference/bson-types), most
  notably in the backup resources. These are represented in JSON (Javascript Object Notation)
  documents as an object with two fields: `date`, which is an
  ISO-8601-formatted date string in UTC with granularity to the second,
  and `increment` a 32-bit integer.

- **Field Names for Fields with Numbers**

  Fields that contain numeric values in a particular unit will be named
  so as to disambiguate the unit being used.

- **Empty Fields**

  Fields that do not have a current value are returned with an
  appropriate default value.

  Fields that do not have a sensible default value are omitted from the
  entity.

- **Field Order**

  The fields in the JSON (Javascript Object Notation) documents returned by the server are in no
  particular order, and the order may change. Do not depend on the
  order of the fields.

sections.heading: Linking
sections.body:
Each resource includes one or more links to sub-resources and/or
related resources. Links are placed in the `links` field of an
entity, which is an array of link relation objects. Each link relation
has two fields:

| Field | Description                                                                                                                        |
| ----- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| rel   | Name (or type) of the relation. Many of these are considered Extension Relation Types and are prefixed by http\://mms.mongodb.com. |
| href  | The target URL (Uniform Resource Locator).                                                                                         |

All entities include at least one link relation called `self`, which
is simply its own URL (Uniform Resource Locator). When an entity is part of a list, then it only
includes the `self` link relation.

For more information, refer to the [Web Linking Specification](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc8288). Note that although the
specification describes a format for including links in the HTTP (HyperText Transport Protocol)
response headers, doing so is not a requirement. To make the
Atlas Administration API easily browsable, it includes the links in the
response body rather than in the response headers.

sections.heading: Lists
sections.body:
Some resources return a list of entities. When a list of entities is
expected in a response, the results are returned in batches bounded by
the following query parameters:

| Field        | Description                                                                                     |
| ------------ | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| pageNum      | Page number (1-based). Defaults to 1 if not specified.                                          |
| itemsPerPage | Number of items to return per page, up to a maximum of 500. Defaults to 100 if not specified.   |
| includeCount | Specifies whether the response returns the totalCount field. Defaults to true if not specified. |

The response entity contains three fields:

| Field      | Description                                                                                                                                                                                                                  |
| ---------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| totalCount | The total number of items in the entire result set.                                                                                                                                                                          |
| results    | The result set, which is an array of entity documents.                                                                                                                                                                       |
| links      | Contains one to three link relations: previous for the previous page of results (omitted for the first page); next for the next page of results (omitted for the last page); and self for the current page (always present). |

If you make a request for a list of entities and there are no results,
then the Atlas Administration API responds with a 200 status code and the `results`
array is empty. It does *not* respond with a 404 in this case, since
the list of entities may not be empty at some point in the future.
However, if you request a list of entities in a context that does not
exist (e.g., the list of hosts for a non-existent project), then this
*does* result in a 404 response status.

sections.heading: Envelopes
sections.body:
Some clients might not be able to access the HTTP (HyperText Transport Protocol) response headers
and/or status code. In that case, you can request that the response
include an "envelope," which is simply an extra layer of information in
the JSON (Javascript Object Notation) document and contains any relevant details that would
normally be in the response headers. By default, the Atlas Administration API
does *not* include the response in an envelope. To request one, simply
add the query parameter `envelope=true`.

For responses that contain a single entity, the envelope contains two
fields:

| Field   | Description                                          |
| ------- | ---------------------------------------------------- |
| status  | The HTTP (HyperText Transport Protocol) status code. |
| content | The requested entity.                                |

For responses that contain a list of entities, there is already an
envelope that wraps the results, so specifying `envelope=true` only
adds the `status` field to the existing envelope.

sections.heading: Pretty Printing
sections.body:
By default, extraneous whitespace is stripped from the JSON (Javascript Object Notation) returned
by the server. To ask for pretty-printed JSON (Javascript Object Notation), simply append the
`pretty=true` query parameter to any request:

```shell
curl --header 'Authorization: Bearer {ACCESS-TOKEN}' \
  --header 'Accept: application/json' \
  --include \
  --request GET "https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/atlas/v1.0?pretty=true"
```

sections.heading: Response Codes
sections.body:
Responses use the standard HTTP (HyperText Transport Protocol) response codes, including:

| Code | Meaning               | Notes                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
| ---- | --------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| 200  | OK                    | The request was successful. This is typically the response to a successful GET request.                                                                                                                                                                                                                    |
| 201  | Created               | A new resource was created. This is typically the response to a successful POST request.                                                                                                                                                                                                                   |
| 202  | Accepted              | A request for an asynchronous operation was accepted.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
| 400  | Bad Request           | Something was wrong with the client request.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               |
| 401  | Unauthorized          | Authentication is required but was not present in the request. Typically this means that the authentication information was omitted from the request, the provided credentials are incorrect, or the user associated with the given authentication method is not allowed to access the requested resource. |
| 403  | Forbidden             | Access to the specified resource is not permitted.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         |
| 404  | Not Found             | The requested resource does not exist.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     |
| 405  | Method Not Allowed    | The HTTP (HyperText Transport Protocol) method is not supported for the specified resource. Keep in mind that each resource may only support a subset of HTTP (HyperText Transport Protocol) methods. For example, you can't DELETE the root resource.                                                     |
| 409  | Conflict              | This is the response to a request to create or modify a property of an entity that is unique when an existing entity already exists with the same value for that property.                                                                                                                                 |
| 5xx  | Various server errors | Something unexpected went wrong. Try again later and consider notifying Atlas Support.                                                                                                                                                                                                                     |

sections.heading: Errors
sections.body:
When a request returns an error, the response body contains a JSON (Javascript Object Notation)
document. This document includes details about why the
Atlas Administration API request failed. The document contains five
parameters:

| Parameter  | Data Type | Description                                                                                                                                           |
| ---------- | --------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| detail     | string    | Human-readable description that Atlas returns for the errant Atlas Administration API request.                                                        |
| error      | integer   | Status code returned in the header of the HTTP (HyperText Transport Protocol) response.                                                               |
| errorCode  | string    | Named constant that represents the errant Atlas Administration API request. To learn about these constants, see Atlas Administration API Error Codes. |
| parameters | array     | List of parameters that provide further details about the error.                                                                                      |
| reason     | string    | Status code definition returned in the header of the HTTP (HyperText Transport Protocol) response.                                                    |

> **Example:** Atlas returns the following response body when you make a
> request in the incorrect format:
>
> ```json
> {
>   "detail" : "Cannot find resource /api/atlas/v1.0/softwareComponents/version.",
>   "error" : 404,
>   "errorCode" : "RESOURCE_NOT_FOUND",
>   "parameters" : [ "/api/atlas/v1.0/softwareComponents/version" ],
>   "reason" : "Not Found"
> }
> ```

sections.heading: Project ID
sections.body:
Your Project ID is a string value that uniquely identifies a
Atlas project.

Atlas projects were previously identified as "groups". Some
Atlas endpoints reference `group` or `{GROUP-ID}` as part of
the request path, query, or body parameters. For any endpoint that
requires your `{GROUP-ID}`, specify your Project ID instead.

To retrieve your project ID:

sections.heading: Authentication
sections.body:
The Atlas Administration API uses one of two methods to authenticate
requests: service accounts or API (Application Programming Interface) keys. This ensures that the access tokens and keys
that serve as usernames and passwords are never sent over the network.
To learn more, see [Atlas Administration API Authentication Methods](api-authentication).

For usage details, see [Make an API (Application Programming Interface) Request](prog-access-usage).

> **Important:** We recommend you use service accounts instead of API (Application Programming Interface) keys to
> authenticate to the Atlas Administration API. API (Application Programming Interface) keys are a legacy
> authentication method.

sections.heading: Rate Limiting
sections.body:
Atlas uses *rate limiting* to control the rate of requests sent to the
V2 API (Application Programming Interface) endpoints within a specified timeframe to prevent overload and
ensure fair usage. Atlas uses the Token Bucket Algorithm to limit the number
of requests it processes. To learn more, see [rate limiting](api-rate-limiting).

sections.heading: Next Steps
sections.body:
To get started, see [API Access Lists endpoint](atlas-admin-api-access).

To manage programmatic access to the Atlas Administration API, see
any of the following procedures:

- [Manage Programmatic Access to an Organization](atlas-admin-api-access-org).

- [Manage Programmatic Access to Multiple Organizations](prog-access-mult-orgs).

- [Manage Programmatic Access to a Project](atlas-admin-api-access-project).
